era_context: Late Middle Bronze Age. This year marks the traditional starting point of Hammurabi's reign in the 'middle chronology.' Egypt's 13th Dynasty was in decline. The Hittite Old Kingdom was consolidating in Anatolia. Mycenaean Greece was in the Middle Helladic period. The broader era was characterized by the rise of territorial kingdoms and the decline of earlier city-state systems.

events.title: Accession of Hammurabi to the throne of Babylon
events.description: Hammurabi ascended to the throne of Babylon, traditionally dated to 1792 BCE in the 'middle chronology.' He inherited a city-state of moderate power and began a reign that would transform Babylon into the dominant power in Mesopotamia. His early years were marked by consolidation of power and the beginning of military campaigns against neighboring city-states and kingdoms.
events.certainty_note: The year 1792 BCE is the conventional starting point for Hammurabi's reign in the 'middle chronology,' but this dating is debated. Low chronology places his accession c. 1728 BCE; high chronology places it c. 1848 BCE. The uncertainty is ±60 years or more.

disconfirming_evidence: The year -1792 as the starting point of Hammurabi's reign is entirely dependent on the 'middle chronology,' which is one of three competing chronological systems for ancient Mesopotamia. The 'low chronology' and 'high chronology' place his accession significantly earlier or later. There is no contemporary astronomical observation or absolute dating method that definitively establishes -1792 as the correct year. The choice of -1792 is conventional, not factual.
historiographic_note: The historical record for Hammurabi's accession consists primarily of later king lists and his own inscriptions and correspondence. There are no contemporary external sources (e.g., Egyptian records) that confirm the date. Babylonian chronology remains one of the most uncertain areas of ancient history, with scholars disagreeing by over a century on the placement of Hammurabi's reign. The surviving sources are heavily biased toward Babylon itself; the perspectives of neighboring kingdoms and regions are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Hammurabi's accession initiated the period of expansion and legal development documented in subsequent years
